data.title: Nonfarm payroll growth is seen slowing but staying 'relatively strong' in June
data.description:
The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics will issue its June Employment Situation report on Thursday instead of the usual Friday, due to observance of the Independence Day holiday. While the Federal Reserve appears to be more focused on inflation than the employment rate recently, the policymakers will still scour the jobs report for signs of economic growth or warnings of weakness.

The U.S. economy is expected to add 114K jobs in June, down from the 172K estimated in May, according to the average estimate of economists. The June report comes after U.S. nonfarm payrolls showed surprisingly strong growth in the past three months, leading many economists to believe the labor market has stabilized.

The unemployment rate is expected to remain at 4.3%. 

The state of the labor market is important because it makes up half of the Federal Reserve's dual mandate — maximum employment and price stability. Its decisions affect the interest rates consumers pay on mortgages and credit card debt and businesses pay on loans and debt to fund expansion.

Nicole Bachaud, a labor economist at jobs site ZipRecruiter, expects a "relatively strong report" with the economy adding 120K-150K jobs in June and unemployment remaining stable.

Major labor trends established earlier this year are expected to continue. Those include labor force participation continuing to fall, job growth broadening across sectors, and employers adjusting to last year's elevated macro uncertainty, she said.

 "We're seeing a lot of renewed resilience from employers, especially getting into the summer season," Bachaud said in an interview with Seeking Alpha. "We've seen a big bump in leisure and hospitality employment for the past couple of months." 

In addition, ZipRecruiter is noticing an increase in summer job postings and temporary positions. "There is this emphasis on this summer of performing quite well, and we've seen the data leading up to that point," she added.

The number of seasonal temporary jobs looks strong for the summer, but in looking ahead, Bachaud looks at temporary services help. "There has been a slight increase in temporary help service employment across the economy, which usually is a sign that things are going to continue to grow," she said.

While almost all of last year's employment growth came from the health care sector, this year other sectors are also contributing. Bachaud points to skilled trades, construction (fueled by the AI infrastructure boom), leisure and hospitality, and business and professional services as strengthening sectors.

"We've really seen this broadening of the labor market away from a single industry dominance and towards more distributed growth," she said.

With the labor market strengthening, the declining labor force participation rate could signal labor market tightening ahead. "That could be putting some pressure on the market as we continue to see job growth happening and fewer people available in the market to work those jobs," Bachaud said.

A factor that could offset the waning participation rate is a lag in employers hiring. ZipRecruiter is seeing hesitation in businesses hiring for open positions. Job openings have increased for the past two months, but the hiring rate has stayed low, she said. Part of it may be related to expectations of increased productivity related to the adoption of artificial intelligence. 

"People who are working in AI-specific fields and roles are seeing more job offers and activity from employers, but they're also seeing more offers being rescinded," as potential employers rethink their labor needs, Bachaud said.

The BLS's Employment Situation report will also provide fresh data on workers' wages. The ZipRecruiter economist highlighted the trend that wage growth is not keeping up with inflation, which could impact household spending.

Average hourly earnings are expected to rise 0.3% M/M, according to the consensus estimate, unchanged from the 0.3% rate it logged in the prior month. On a Y/Y basis, that's anticipated to be 3.5%, accelerating a tick from 3.4% in May. By comparison, headline PCE inflation rose to 4.1% in May.

"Inflation is the thing that could delay growth from continuing at the same pace," Bachaud said.

That has implications for Fed policy. "The expectation that there are probably going to be rate hikes later this year is in line with a stabilized labor market and an increasing level of inflation. Those things are expected now and kind of baked in."

Not everyone expects June's numbers to sustain the strength seen in previous months. Citi's Veronica Clark expects the U.S. economy to add only about 25K jobs due to a low hiring rate, a drop in small business hiring plans, declining Indeed.com job postings, and rising initial jobless claims during the June reference period.

"A number of other weaker data points – most importantly rising initial and continuing jobless claims – lead us to think that strength in payroll data is not a sign of sustainably stronger demand for workers," Clark wrote in a note to clients.

data.title: Tarsus Pharmaceuticals falls amid Culper Research short call
data.description:
• Tarsus Pharmaceuticals (TARS) dropped 7.8% amid a short report from Culper Research.
  • Culper Research disclosed that it's short shares of Tarsus (TARS).
  • Tarsus didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's email request for comment.
  • Tarsus (TARS) has a short interest of 11.6%.

data.title: Apple could face $52M fine in Russia over alleged bias against local apps: report
data.description:
Russia's Federal Antimonopoly Service urged Apple (AAPL) to address what ​it described as discriminatory ⁠practices against Russian ​search engines and ​software, Reuters reported.

The antimonopoly watchdog said the iPhone maker must ensure Russian ​software, including ​search engines and messenger Max, ‌is ⁠pre-installed on its devices, the report added.

If Apple fails to remedy ​the violations ​by ⁠July 15, it could face ​a fine ​of ⁠up to 4B roubles (about $51.6M), the report noted.

Apple did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Apple had officially halted all product sales and exports to Russia in March 2022.

data.title: OTTR, OKLO draw highest short interest in utilities sector; BIP and RNW see lowest short interest
data.description:
Within the utilities sector, short sellers remain heavily concentrated in electric utilities, with several electricity producers and multi-utility companies ranking among the most heavily shorted stocks in the market at the end of June.

Otter Tail (OTTR) and Oklo (OKLO) recorded the highest short interest among utilities stocks with a market capitalization of over $2B.

The utilities sector (XLU) comprises companies involved in providing services like electricity, natural gas, and water, among others.

Otter Tail (OTTR) was the most shorted stock in the utilities sector, with short interest at 15.64% of its shares outstanding, followed by Oklo (OKLO) at 12.21%, reflecting elevated bearish positioning in both stocks.

Otter Tail (OTTR) was rated Hold by Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings and Wall Street analysts.

Oklo’s shares have slipped nearly 25% year to date, underperforming the benchmark S&P 500, which has gained over 9.5% in the same period. Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings are bearish, rating OKLO a Hold. On the other hand, Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha are bullish, rating it a Buy.

At the other end of the spectrum, Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) and ReNew Energy Global (RNW) were among the least shorted utilities stocks, with short interest of 0.19% and 1.22%, respectively.

The top five most shorted utilities stocks with a market capitalization above $2B (as a percentage of shares outstanding) are:

  • Otter Tail (OTTR) — 15.64% short interest
  • Oklo (OKLO) — 12.21%
  • H2O America (HTO) — 11.98%
  • Black Hills (BKH) — 11.32%
  • Alliant Energy (LNT) — 9.90%

The five least shorted utilities stocks with a market capitalization above $2B are:

  • PG&E Corporation (PCG) — 1.84% short interest
  • Essential Utilities (WTRG) — 1.66%
  • Sempra (SRE) — 1.60%
  • ReNew Energy Global (RNW) — 1.22%
  • Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) — 0.19%

data.title: Interactive Brokers' daily revenue trades rise 53% Y/Y, 6% M/M in June
data.description:
Interactive Brokers Group (IBKR) on Wednesday said its June daily average revenue trades rose 53% from the prior year and 6% from the previous month to 5.269M.

Client accounts increased 34% from 2025 and 4% from May to 5.185M.

Ending client equity stood at $930.3B, up 40% year-over-year and 1% lower than May.

Ending client margin loan balances stood at $108.5B, 67% higher from last year and 8% from the prior month.

Ending client credit balances of $182.4B increased 27% from 2025 and 1% from May.

data.title: FactSet signals tracking toward high end of FY2026 revenue and EPS ranges while scaling AI and cutting tech workforce roughly 10%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: FactSet (FDS) Q3 FY2026

Management View

  • "Q3 performance was strong with our fifth consecutive quarter of acceleration in organic ASV growth" and "We grew ASV by 7.1% to $2.48 billion across all regions and client types," CEO & Director Sanoke Viswanathan said.
  • Viswanathan tied recent execution to AI-driven client behavior, saying "Over 90% of our top 50 clients are now using 4 or more FactSet AI solutions" and "quarter-over-quarter, overall ASV growth among clients using our AI solutions was 50% higher than for the rest of the book."
  • On productivity actions, Viswanathan said, "With these efficiency gains, we initiated a roughly 10% reduction in our technology workforce and freed up significant capacity to accelerate strategic product development," alongside operational changes including "we have reduced operator touch time for data table extraction by more than 50%."
  • Viswanathan introduced "FactSet Intelligence" and highlighted distribution/AI infrastructure progress, including "FactSet's MCP server built on a robust ecosystem of content APIs has over 450 clients actively engaged under contracts and trials" and "API call volume is experiencing rapid growth with Q3 volumes at 13x the level we experienced in Q2."
  • On partnerships and distribution, Viswanathan said, "we announced a strategic partnership with Google Cloud this week, expanding our distribution through Google Cloud's enterprise channels and unlocking new revenue opportunities."
  • "Revenue was $622.9 million" and "Adjusted EPS grew 6.1% to $4.53," CFO Joshua Warren said, adding "our adjusted operating income was $211.8 million, representing a 34% margin."

Outlook

  • "We remain confident in the guidance ranges that were previously set for ASV, revenue, operating margin and EPS," Warren said, adding, "On revenue and EPS in particular, we are tracking toward the high end of those ranges based on our business trajectory."
  • On implied Q4 moderation questions, Viswanathan said "the momentum continues into Q4," while also noting "it is a tough compare" because "Q4 of last year was our largest quarter ever," and flagged execution/timing: "There are multiple 7-figure deals outstanding. So it will be all down to execution in the next 8 weeks, and there could be a timing issue there."

Financial Results

  • The company reported "ASV exceeded $2.48 billion" with "7.1% organic growth" and "revenue was $622.9 million," Warren said.
  • Profitability and cost drivers were described as investment- and incentive-led: "Adjusted operating margin was 34%" and was "down approximately 300 basis points" year-over-year "due to targeted investments" and "performance-related compensation linked to ASV momentum," Warren said.
  • Expense mix and one-time items included: "Compensation-related expenses typically account for approximately 60% of our total cost base," and "our FX hedging program... went from a gain in Q3 2025 to a loss in Q3 2026, creating an overall drag of approximately 60 basis points," Warren said.
  • Cash flow and capital return were highlighted: "Our free cash flow grew to $254 million" and "we accelerated our repurchase activity, buying back approximately 926,000 shares for $203 million," with "over $625 million" returned year-to-date via dividends and repurchases, Warren said.
  • Balance sheet positioning: "gross debt leverage at 1.5x and net debt leverage at 1.2x," and "Fitch reaffirmed" the rating "with a stable outlook," Warren said.

Q&A

  • Ashish Sabadra, RBC Capital Markets: "the guidance implies a moderation in the fourth quarter"; CEO Viswanathan: "we are ahead of last year in terms of our bookings" but "we don't change guidance quarter-to-quarter" and cited "multiple 7-figure deals outstanding" that could create "a timing issue."
  • Faiza Alwy, Deutsche Bank: asked about "AI monetization"; CEO Viswanathan: "over 10% of the ASV growth came directly from AI SKUs" and "over 20% of our top 100 clients are using MCP on a paid basis."
  • Alex Kramm, UBS: asked about margin cadence and "onetime-ish" items; CEO Viswanathan: "we see a clear line of sight now on margin improvement" and "we still have confidence" in focusing "on the midpoint of the guide"; CFO Warren: "the main dynamic in the quarter is timing, specifically around pay-for-performance arrangements."
  • Kelsey Zhu, Autonomous: asked about MCP revenue/user personas; CEO Viswanathan: "whenever there are deals that involve an MCP component... we've seen contract value improvements" and "around 20% of the users of our endpoints are net new users."
  • Manav Patnaik, Barclays: asked if partnerships are "step 1" to M&A; CEO Viswanathan: "It's a deliberate strategy"; CFO Warren: "we intend to be very surgical" and expect inorganic activity to be "derisked" by prior integration work.
  • Shlomo Rosenbaum, Stifel: asked about longer contracts and disclosures; CEO Viswanathan: "we are not taking any price compression in return for their contract extensions"; CFO Warren: "our user count is up 12% year-over-year" and said they will provide "the metrics that matter."
  • Surinder Thind, Jefferies: asked about product portfolio review and M&A fit; CEO Viswanathan: "we are continuing to review our product portfolio against appropriate hurdle rates" and described "the transition from a traditional workstation to an AI native or an agentic workstation."
  • Yehuda Silverman, Morgan Stanley: asked about payback period; CEO Viswanathan: sales/marketing tooling has "very, very fast payback initiatives that pay back in a matter of months" while core infrastructure "generally tend to take longer."
  • Andrew Nicholas, William Blair: asked about AI in wealth; CEO Viswanathan: there is "a big opportunity" to improve "the adviser experience" and also serve "the end customers' experience"; CFO Warren: "Clients are in different stages of evolution."
  • Keen Fai Tong, Goldman Sachs: asked about pricing contribution; CEO Viswanathan: "our price increase this quarter was better than what we were able to achieve at the same quarter last year."
  • Jason Haas, Wells Fargo: asked about implied margin improvement; CEO Viswanathan: "if we continue to outperform on our ASV delivery and we want to pay for performance, I think we are retaining the flexibility in the margin range."
  • Curtis Nagle, BofA: asked about token costs; CFO Warren: "all of the token spending is net new" and "we have a series of operational controls around monitoring them."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly skeptical, repeatedly pressing on guidance conservatism and margin cadence, including "the guidance implies a moderation" and questions on "onetime-ish" items and "higher token costs."
  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, using confidence language such as "we remain confident" and "we see a clear line of sight," while also emphasizing execution/timing risk: "there is a lot to execute ahead of us."
  • Versus last quarter, management’s tone shifted from raising ranges in Q2 ("we are raising our ASV revenue and EPS outlook ranges for fiscal 2026") to reaffirming ranges in Q3 ("We remain confident in the guidance ranges that were previously set"), while analysts increased focus on margin cadence and AI monetization specifics.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q3 emphasized AI infrastructure scaling ("Q3 volumes at 13x the level we experienced in Q2") and workforce actions ("a roughly 10% reduction in our technology workforce"), while Q2 emphasized guidance increases ("we are raising our ASV revenue and EPS outlook ranges for fiscal 2026").
  • Management reiterated the contracting shift both quarters; Q3 updated the renewal mix with "the majority of ASV renewed in Q3" as enterprise or 3+ year terms and "Average contract term extended by roughly 30%," consistent with Q2’s statement that renewals "extended in length by more than 30%."
  • Analyst focus broadened from Q2’s business-model transition questions toward Q3’s margin cadence, token costs, and guidance conservatism.

Risks and Concerns

  • Execution and timing risk was explicitly flagged: "multiple 7-figure deals outstanding" that could create "a timing issue," Viswanathan said.
  • Margin variability risk was tied to incentives and investment pace: CFO Warren cited "pay-for-performance arrangements" as the key quarterly dynamic, and CEO Viswanathan said FactSet wants flexibility "if we continue to outperform on our ASV delivery and we want to pay for performance."
  • Cost headwinds included "token spending" that "has increased year-over-year" and FX hedging that created "an overall drag of approximately 60 basis points," Warren said.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q3 as a continuation of accelerating organic ASV growth alongside an AI-led strategy shift toward enterprise agreements, MCP-based data delivery, and agentic workflows, while also acknowledging near-term execution and margin variability tied to performance-based compensation, token costs, and large-deal timing; they reaffirmed the existing fiscal 2026 guidance ranges and said revenue and EPS were tracking toward the high end of those ranges.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Lockheed Martin raised to Buy at Citi; Karman Holdings on positive catalyst watch
data.description:
Lockheed Martin (LMT) up 2.2% in Wednesday's trading as Citi upgrades the defense contractor to Buy from Neutral with a $582 price target, edged up from $571, citing cheap valuation, improving fundamentals, and exposure to fast-growing themes through its MFC segment as reinforcing the case for an attractive risk/reward.

Many defense stocks have registered one of their "worst quarterly declines in modern history," and the pullback is even worse if looked at compared to February peaks, Citi analyst John Godyn says, and Lockheed (LMT) is "a good example of a good defense stock that historically bounces back consistently and sharply after exactly these types of moves lower."

Godyn also issues a positive catalyst watch on Karman Holdings (KRMN), maintaining a Buy rating and $76 price target, calling it one of the best examples in Citi's defense coverage of a stock that could experience "asymmetric upside" if organic growth reaccelerates.

The analyst thinks Karman (KRMN) is set for a meaningful tactical rebound through Q2 earnings, citing valuation that has compressed significantly more than aerospace and defense peers, a high likelihood of a Q2 earnings beat-and-raise, and the company's unique exposure to "key megatrends" that are on track to accelerate through the remainder of 2026 and into 2027.

data.title: Bending Spoons rallies more than 10% in its IPO debut
data.description:
Bending Spoons (BSP) trades above its IPO pricing level after debuting on the Nasdaq. The company traded as high as $33.14 after pricing its IPO at $29.00 per share. The stock was swapping hands at $32.81 at last check.

The IPO consists of a total of 57.97M ordinary shares. Certain selling shareholders are offering another 23.57M shares. Bending Spoons (BS) highlighted that it will not receive any proceeds from the secondary portion of the sale. The company and selling shareholders have granted underwriters a 30-day option to purchase up to an additional 8.70M shares (5.24M from the company and 3.45M from the selling shareholders) at the public offering price.

Founded in 2013, Bending Spoons (BSP) follows a digital brand roll-up model, acquiring underperforming but well-known software and app businesses and trying to revive them via product optimization, subscription monetization, and heavy use of AI and data science. The company owns the Vimeo, WeTransfer, Evernote, Eventbrite, and AOL businesses.

data.title: Microsoft, Amazon seen 'standing out' from strong cloud spending, Jefferies says
data.description:
Microsoft (MSFT) and Amazon (AMZN) are “standing out” in a recent survey showing strong cloud computing spending for the rest of 2026, Jefferies said.

“Cloud spend will modestly accelerate to 10.1% in '26 vs. 9.6% in '25 based on survey data, vs. our previous cloud survey, which implied a slight deceleration,” Jefferies analysts wrote in a note to clients. “~95% of respondents expect their cloud budgets to grow y/y in '26. 53% expect their cloud budgets to grow by single digits, while 43% anticipate a double-digit increase. Only 3% of survey respondents anticipate a marginal decrease in '26 cloud spend, consistent w/our previous survey.”

Additionally, the survey, which interviewed 40 U.S. Chief Information Officers, showed that more IT workloads are expected to shift towards the cloud in the next couple of years. Eighty-five percent of respondents said that more than half of workloads would be on the cloud by the end of 2027, up from 75% of respondents. And 53% of respondents expect cloud workloads to be more than 80% by 2027, “a significant step-up from 25% seeing those levels today,” the analysts added.

The survey also showed the major cloud service providers—Azure, AWS, and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) Cloud—as being the biggest winners from AI adoption, a consistent figure from past surveys.  

Other takeaways

The survey also provided other takeaways, including the fact that Microsoft's Azure extended its lead over AWS as the preferred cloud vendor for the group of CIOs.

“Azure is the primary Cloud provider for 55% of respondents in this survey vs. AWS at 28%, a 27pt gap that has widened from just 7pts in Dec '25 (Azure 45% / AWS 38%),” the analysts wrote. “Spend allocation tells the same story, w/ the gap widening to 17pts (Azure 46% / AWS 29%) from 3pts last survey (Azure 40% / AWS 37%). While the directional widening on both metrics signals Azure momentum, we believe all 3 hyperscalers can benefit as AI demand continues to exceed supply.”

Additionally, 68% of respondents said they expect Azure spending to increase over the next 24 months, while only 45% said they expect AWS spending to increase and just 25% for Google Cloud.

“Notably, GCP's net spending score for CY26 has slipped to 35% from 52% in Dec '25, though we'd flag the smaller sample size (35% of CIOs marked GOOG as not applicable),” the analysts explained. “Directionally, the read still signals GCP's continued enterprise push as the dark horse in cloud.”

data.title: Stellantis North American June vehicle sales increase 10%
data.description:
• Stellantis (STLA) sold 634,187 vehicles during the first six months of 2026, an increase of 5% year-over-year led by increased demand for the Jeep Grand Wagoneer.
  • Sales for the month of June alone were up 10% and up 6% for the second quarter compared to the same period last year.
  • The sales trajectory sets the company up to achieve its ambitious FaSTLAne goal of 25% revenue growth and 35% volume through 2030 with a 50% increase in market coverage.
  • After bottoming out at a 6-year low on Monday, Stellantis (STLA) shares are in the green for a second day.
